posts.topic: The building at 112 Prince Street in SoHo hides 150 years of secrets behind its cast-iron facade
posts.headline: This building has been hiding a secret for 150 years.
posts.subtext: 112 Prince Street. Cast-iron facade. What's behind the door that nobody opens?

posts.topic: Grand Central Terminal has a secret platform (Track 61) used by President Roosevelt
posts.fact_text: Grand Central has a secret platform called Track 61 — built so President Roosevelt could enter the Waldorf Astoria without being seen in his wheelchair.

posts.topic: West Village: the narrowest house in NYC is at 75½ Bedford Street — just 9.5 feet wide
posts.headline: The narrowest house in New York is 9.5 feet wide.
posts.subtext: 75½ Bedford Street. Three stories. One secret. Built in 1873 in a former carriage alley.

posts.topic: What happens when your phone sends you into a hidden alley in Midtown at 3PM
posts.headline: Your phone says: turn left into the alley. Now.
posts.subtext: 3:14 PM. Midtown. The chat sends you somewhere Google Maps doesn't show.

posts.topic: There is a whispering gallery in Grand Central where secrets travel across the ceiling
posts.headline: Stand in the corner. Whisper. Someone 30 feet away hears every word.
posts.subtext: The Whispering Gallery. Grand Central. A 100-year-old acoustic secret hiding in plain sight.

posts.topic: Below Bryant Park lies an entire underground extension of the NY Public Library
posts.fact_text: Below Bryant Park lies an entire underground library — 84,000 sq ft of book stacks that most New Yorkers walk over every day without knowing.

posts.topic: Harlem: the Cotton Club, Strivers Row, and jazz legends that still haunt the streets
posts.headline: The jazz never stopped. You just stopped listening.
posts.subtext: The Cotton Club. Strivers' Row. Langston Hughes' front stoop. Harlem's ghosts play on.

posts.topic: The city is not a place. It is a puzzle waiting to be decoded.
posts.quote:
The city is not a place.
It's a puzzle waiting to be decoded.

posts.topic: A hidden bar behind an unmarked door in the Flatiron District has been serving drinks since 1854
posts.headline: There's no sign. No name. Just a door that's been open since 1854.
posts.subtext: Flatiron District. An unmarked entrance. The oldest continually operating bar in NYC.

posts.topic: The Brooklyn Bridge has a secret wine cellar built into its stone arches
posts.fact_label: the year they sealed the vaults
posts.fact_text: Inside the Brooklyn Bridge's stone arches are hidden wine cellars — used to store champagne and wine to help fund the bridge's construction.

posts.topic: DUMBO: the Manhattan Bridge view that every photographer knows — but the real secrets are underground
posts.headline: Everyone photographs the bridge. Nobody looks underneath.
posts.subtext: DUMBO. Empire Stores. Jane's Carousel. And tunnels that predate the borough.

posts.topic: You get a message: Follow the mosaic trail. The first clue is under your feet.
posts.headline: Follow the mosaic trail. The first clue is under your feet.
posts.subtext: The chat knows where you are. The city is the game board. Look down.

posts.topic: There is an abandoned subway station directly beneath City Hall — and you can see it if you know how
posts.headline: There's a subway station under City Hall. It's been closed since 1945.
posts.subtext: Stained glass. Brass chandeliers. Guastavino tiles. All underground. All abandoned.

posts.topic: The Chrysler Building lobby has a ceiling mural that almost nobody looks up to see
posts.fact_label: feet of painted ceiling above you
posts.fact_text: The Chrysler Building lobby has a massive Art Deco mural on its ceiling — depicting industry, progress, and the building itself. Almost nobody looks up.

posts.topic: Chelsea: 200+ art galleries in 10 blocks, and a former Nabisco factory turned food hall
posts.headline: 200 galleries in 10 blocks. And a cookie factory turned food hall.
posts.subtext: Chelsea. Where Nabisco baked Oreos and now the art world decides what matters.

posts.topic: Every building has a story. Most of them are classified.
posts.quote:
Every building has a story.
Most of them are classified.

posts.topic: Somewhere in Tribeca, the original Ghostbusters firehouse still stands — and it is still in use
posts.headline: The Ghostbusters firehouse is real. And it's still in use.
posts.subtext: Hook & Ladder 8. North Moore Street. Tribeca. Still answering calls since 1903.

posts.topic: Central Park has a hidden cave called The Ramble that most New Yorkers have never found
posts.fact_text: Central Park's Ramble is a 38-acre wild forest with a hidden cave — most New Yorkers have walked past it thousands of times without ever going in.

posts.topic: Greenwich Village: where the Beat Generation wrote, the Stonewall uprising started, and Bob Dylan played his first NYC gig
posts.headline: Three revolutions started within 6 blocks of each other.
posts.subtext: The Beats. Stonewall. Bob Dylan at Café Wha?. Greenwich Village rewrote America.

posts.topic: The chat says: Look up. The answer is in the architecture. You have 90 seconds.
posts.headline: Look up. The answer is in the architecture. You have 90 seconds.
posts.subtext: The clock is ticking. The chat sent you here for a reason. The gargoyle knows.
